turns.content: I think I need to set a boundary with my sister, but I don't know how. We talk every day and lately... I dread it.
That dread — what is it made of?

*pauses*

I think... it's like, I pick up the phone and I already know what's coming. She's going to need something from me. Emotionally, I mean. And I'll give it, I always give it, but afterward I feel kind of... emptied out. 

But she's my sister, so. I don't know if that's a fair thing to even say.

That last part — "I don't know if that's a fair thing to even say."

What makes it unfair?

*small pause*

I think... she's had a hard time. She has. And I've always been the one who... I mean, that's just kind of how our family works. She's older but I'm the one people come to. So it feels like -- I don't know, like complaining about something that's just my job, almost.

*quieter*

Which I know sounds a little strange when I say it out loud.

Yeah. It does sound a little strange. Stay with that for a second.

What does it cost you — this job?

*takes a breath*

I think... it costs me a lot of evenings. Like, I'll have plans to do something for myself and then she calls and I just... don't do the thing. And it's not that she's forcing me, she's not. I just can't seem to -- 

*pauses*

And I'm tired. I think I'm more tired than I've been letting myself notice.

But I also don't want to make her sound like a villain, because she's not. She's just... she needs a lot, and I've always been available for that, so she doesn't know any different. That's not her fault.

The tiredness — let it be there for a moment. You don't have to explain it away.

What would it mean if it was just true — that you're tired, and it costs a lot — without any of the "but she's not a villain" attached to it?

*sits with that for a moment*

I think... it would mean I actually want something different. Like, I want to talk to her less. Not never -- I love her. But less.

*quickly*

Which, I mean, that's okay to want, right? I think that's okay.

*pause, softer*

It's just that when I actually say it that plainly it feels like... I don't know. Like I'm betraying something. Our whole family kind of runs on me being the one who holds things together, and if I'm saying I don't want to do that as much anymore, then what does that make me?

*long pause*

Cold, I think. Like... selfish. 

*quieter*

My mom used to say I was the strong one. And I think I took that in as -- I don't know, as like a responsibility. Not just a compliment. So if I'm saying I don't want to be that as much... 

*trails off, then*

I guess it feels like I'm letting everyone down. Even though rationally I know that's not -- I mean, I know that's not quite right. But it's what it feels like.

That's not irrational. That's a very old instruction.

"The strong one" — you took it in as a job description. And now wanting less feels like quitting.

What would it mean about you if you weren't the strong one anymore?

*pause, breath held slightly*

I think it would mean... I was ordinary? Like, that sounds so strange to say. But I think part of me has been -- the strong one is how I matter in this family. It's how I'm needed. And if I'm not that...

*stops*

I don't know what I am to them.

*small laugh, a little uncomfortable*

That's a bigger thing than I came in here to talk about. I came in here to figure out what to say to my sister. Like, literally what words to use.

Yeah. The words were never really the problem, were they.

*pause*

What's it like to have just said that — that you don't know what you are to them without the role?
